---
title: "2 · Descriptives"
subtitle: "Summarise the sample and look before you model"
date: today
---

```{r setup}
#| include: false
source(file.path(rprojroot::find_root(rprojroot::is_git_root), "R", "01_setup.R"))
clean <- get_clean_consumer_data()   # cached file from page 1, or rebuilt if absent
```

This page reads the cleaned dataset produced by
[1 · Data preparation](01-data-preparation.qmd) and does two things every
analysis should do before any model: **describe the sample** and **plot the
patterns**.

## Outcomes by condition

`descriptives_by_condition()` lives in
[`R/functions/analysis.R`](https://github.com/TheDataFlowCompany/the-science-repository/blob/main/R/functions/analysis.R)
and is shared with the manuscript, so the numbers can't drift between the two.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-desc
#| tbl-cap: "Means and SDs of the key outcomes, by experimental condition."
knitr::kable(descriptives_by_condition(clean), digits = 2)
```

The `combined_frame` row already stands out on `mean_pi` (purchase intention).
The models on the next page test whether that difference holds up.

## Look first

```{r}
#| label: fig-pi-by-cond
#| fig-cap: "Purchase intention by condition. Red points are condition means; bars are 95% CIs."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4
plot_pi_by_condition(clean)
```

Why might `combined_frame` do better? The scatter below is the clue: the frames
also raise **perceived value**, and perceived value tracks purchase intention
closely — the setup for a mediation story.

```{r}
#| label: fig-mediation-scatter
#| fig-cap: "Perceived value (the mediator) versus purchase intention, coloured by condition."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4
plot_mediation_scatter(clean)
```

Next: [3 · Analysis](03-analysis.qmd).
